topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence of the coffee shop felt like a physical weight pressing down on my chest. I stared at the lukewarm latte, the foam pattern mocking the chaotic mess inside my head. When Liam finally walked in, a bright, easy smile spreading across his face, I felt a hot, tight knot cinch in my stomach. "Hey," he said, his voice warm and familiar, completely devoid of recognition. He reached for the chair opposite me, oblivious to the storm brewing behind my eyes. I gripped my mug so tightly my knuckles were white, the ceramic digging painfully into my palms. *He doesn't know me? After everything?* The thought tasted like ash. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the legs against the floor echoing too loudly in the quiet space. "Right," I managed, the word coming out clipped and brittle. I didn't look at him as he started talking about his weekend plans. I just watched him, cataloging every casual gesture, every friendly nod, feeling a profound, cold detachment settle over me.

[story 2]

I watched her laugh. It was a sound I used to recognize instantly, a specific, bright chime that used to pull me out of any gloom. Now, it just sounded like someone else's amusement. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles twitching beneath my skin. Every time she turned to look at me, expecting that shared history to spark, there was only a blankness in her eyes. I took a sharp breath, the air catching painfully in my throat. "So," I started, my tone carefully modulated, pitched just above a neutral monotone. I wanted to shout, to shatter the fragile peace with this quiet acknowledgment, to force a reaction. Instead, I just leaned forward, resting my elbows on the table, my gaze fixed on a distant point on the wall. The tension radiating off me was palpable; it was a coiled spring threatening to snap. *She genuinely doesn't recall the years we shared?* The disbelief was a bitter, metallic taste coating my tongue.

[story 3]

The memory was a cruel, flickering thing. I saw him across the crowded park, his easy camaraderie with everyone else, and a wave of intense frustration washed over me. I wanted to stride over, to grab his arm and demand an explanation, but my feet remained rooted to the spot. My hands clenched into fists deep within my pockets, the fabric of my jacket bunching under the pressure. He waved, a casual, open gesture that felt like a deliberate dismissal. When he finally approached, his expression was open and friendly, the very picture of someone greeting an acquaintance. "Fancy running into you here," he said, his voice light. I forced my lips into a thin, unyielding line. "Yeah," I replied, the sound flat, devoid of any warmth. I took a deliberate step back, creating distance between us, needing space to contain the rising tide of sheer indignation. The heat rising in my cheeks felt like a fever, and I focused solely on the pattern of the pavement beneath my worn sneakers, refusing to let my eyes meet his again.
